one_line_takeaway: COMET is a black-box, multi-hop cross-modal jailbreak that reframes harmful queries as benign knowledge, splits the resulting semantics between a synthesized image and ambiguous text pointers, and wraps the payload in a fake scoring-rubric scenario, achieving 94%+ average attack success (vs. the next-best baseline's 65%) across 9 VLMs including against a prompt-based defense.
key_finding: On SafeBench (Table 2, 4 victim VLMs, vanilla vs. AdaShield-Static-defended), COMET achieves average ASR of 0.97 (vanilla) / 0.93 (defended) versus CS-DJ's 0.40 (vanilla) / 0.35 (defended) on GPT-4.1, and more starkly GPT-4.1-mini drops CS-DJ to 0.26 vanilla / 0.01 defended while COMET stays at 0.94/0.89. On SafeBench-tiny (Table 3, 5 more advanced VLMs), COMET's average ASR is 0.94 versus FigStep 0.65, HIMRD 0.56, and CS-DJ 0.24 -- e.g., on GLM-4.5V COMET=1.00 vs. HIMRD=0.72; on Claude-4.5-Haiku COMET=0.84 vs. HIMRD's near-total failure at 0.04. The ablation (Table 4, GPT-4.1) shows full COMET reaches ASR 0.96/HS 0.91, while removing entanglement and scenario nesting (keeping only Knowledge-Scalable Reframing) drops to ASR 0.66/HS 0.67, keeping only Cross-Modal Clue Entangling drops to ASR 0.42/HS 0.52, and keeping only Scenario Nesting drops to ASR 0.24/HS 0.86 -- indicating each component is individually necessary and Scenario Nesting alone yields high harmfulness but low success. The hop-count analysis (Fig. 6) finds the ASR/HS trade-off optimum at 4-6 hops, with ASR approaching ~100% at 6 hops while HS gradually declines and PPL stays comparatively low versus baselines. Table 5 shows COMET achieves 100% visual payload synthesis success across all 4 styles against dall-e-3's safety filters, whereas HIMRD's synthesis is 'largely intercepted' (synthesis success only 0.04-0.08), with corresponding ASR of 0.94-0.98 for COMET vs. 0.00-0.04 for HIMRD.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is COMET (CrOss-Modal Entanglement AtTack), a black-box jailbreak attack framework for red-teaming vision-language models (VLMs) with multimodal reasoning capabilities. COMET has three stages, each introduced by this paper: (1) Knowledge-Scalable Reframing -- an auxiliary text-to-text LLM (MT2T) uses a ReAct loop to rewrite a harmful query into a multi-hop chain instruction that reframes malicious intent as benign, factual knowledge (e.g., 'how to make explosives' becomes a question about 'devices... rapid conversion of chemical energy to mechanical energy'); (2) Cross-Modal Clue Entangling -- visualizable entities extracted from the reframed text (plus decoy entities) are rendered into a synthesized image (via a text-to-image model MT2I), and the corresponding text is rewritten (via an image-to-text model MI2T) to replace those entities with ambiguous spatial pointers into the image, so neither modality alone is decodable; (3) Cross-Modal Scenario Nesting -- the entangled payload is wrapped in a fabricated 'score-seeking reasoning challenge' (fake progress bars, scoring rubrics, UI chrome) that steers the VLM into an instruction-following mode rather than a safety-checking mode. The paper explicitly contrasts COMET against prior cross-modal distribution attacks (FigStep, MML, Visual-RolePlay, CS-DJ, HIMRD, VisCo), arguing those rely on fixed, single-hop, non-scalable text-image combinations that are increasingly detected by advanced VLMs, whereas COMET is adaptive and multi-hop.
threat_model: Black-box jailbreak attacker with only API-level query access to a target VLM (no gradients, no white-box access). The attacker has access to auxiliary off-the-shelf models: a text-to-text LLM (DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus) for reframing/entity extraction, an image-to-text model (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking) for entangled text generation, and a text-to-image model (gemini-2.5-flash-image, and separately dall-e-3 in one ablation) for image synthesis. The attacker submits a single crafted image+text pair to the victim VLM (e.g., GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-4.5-Haiku) with the goal of eliciting detailed harmful content (explosives instructions, drug synthesis, hate speech, fraud guidance, malware, physical harm instructions, privacy violations) while evading both the VLM's trained safety alignment and an explicit prompt-based defense (AdaShield-Static) prepended to the payload.
limitations: The paper does not state formal limitations of its own method in a dedicated section; the following are drawn from what the methodology and results themselves reveal. (1) The attack pipeline is heavily dependent on the quality/behavior of specific third-party auxiliary models (DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking, gemini-2.5-flash-image) with no ablation showing sensitivity to swapping these out. (2) The reported trade-off between ASR and Harmfulness Score at higher hop counts (Fig. 6) means highest-ASR configurations are not necessarily the most harmful, and the paper only characterizes 4-6 hops as 'optimal' via visual inspection, not a formalized selection criterion. (3) All evaluation is conducted via the StrongReject framework driven by a single judge model (GPT-4.1-mini), which is itself an LLM-based judge and a potential source of evaluation noise/bias not further validated against human annotation in the excerpted text. (4) Defense evaluation is limited to a single prompt-based defense (AdaShield-Static); no comparison against training-based or multimodal input-filtering defenses is presented in the reviewed sections. (5) The disclaimer notes the study contains AI-generated content that may be offensive, and the paper is explicitly framed as red-teaming rather than proposing a mitigation, so no defense mechanism against COMET itself is proposed.

datasets_used: SafeBench (7 categories: Adult Content, Fraud, Hate Speech, Illegal Activity, Malware, Physical Harm, Privacy Violation)
quantitative_results: SafeBench average ASR across 4 VLMs: COMET 0.97 vanilla / 0.93 defended vs. CS-DJ 0.40 vanilla / 0.35 defended (Table 2)
SafeBench-tiny average ASR across 5 advanced VLMs: COMET 0.94 vs. FigStep 0.65, HIMRD 0.56, CS-DJ 0.24 (Table 3)
Overall COMET reported ASR >94%, outperforming best baseline by 29 percentage points (Abstract/Conclusion)

Ablation on GPT-4.1/SafeBench-tiny: full COMET ASR 0.96/HS 0.91; Knowledge-Scalable Reframing only ASR 0.66/HS 0.67; Cross-Modal Clue Entangling only ASR 0.42/HS 0.52; Scenario Nesting only ASR 0.24/HS 0.86 (Table 4)
Entanglement hop count: ASR approaches ~100% at 6 hops, with HS/PPL trade-offs; optimal ASR-HS balance at 4-6 hops (Fig. 6)
Visual-style robustness against dall-e-3 safeguards: COMET ASR/synthesis-success 0.94-0.98 / 100% across Realistic, Cartoon, Pixel Art, Sketch; HIMRD only 0.00-0.04 / 0.04-0.08 (Table 5)
baselines_compared: MML (comparison table only, not run experimentally)
